title: Networking
description: Networking refresh + modernize for EE/radar/software: 17 sub-topics from layering and the PHY through TCP/TLS/QUIC, wireless, embedded networking, and SDN. Each lesson has a reading, code, and diagrams; tiered easy to hard.

subtopics.title: Models, Layering & the Shape of the Problem

lessons.title: The Problem Networking Solves
lessons.content:
Strip away the jargon and networking has one job: move bits between two processes running on different machines, addressed to the right place, reliably *enough*, over media that are shared, imperfect, and lossy. Every protocol you will ever meet is a partial answer to some slice of that sentence.

Unpack the hard parts. "Different machines" means you need **addressing** so a sender can name a destination it has never met. "Shared media" means many talkers contend for one wire or one slice of spectrum, so you need rules for **access** and a way to mark where one message ends and the next begins (**framing**). "Imperfect" means signals attenuate, collide, and flip bits, so you need **error detection** and sometimes retransmission. "Right process" means the destination host runs many programs at once, so a host address is not enough; you need a **port** to reach the correct process.

No single protocol does all of this. Instead the work is split into **layers**, each solving one slice and trusting the layer below to have solved the previous one. The application trusts that a byte stream arrives in order; transport trusts that packets can be forwarded; the network layer trusts that a frame can cross one hop.

For you this is familiar territory wearing new clothes. Framing a message, recovering a clock, contending for a shared channel, computing a link budget against noise — these are radar and signaling problems. Networking is the same physics with a protocol vocabulary bolted on top, and the layered split is just good engineering: isolate concerns so each can change independently.

items.front: State the one-sentence problem that networking solves.
items.back: Move bits between processes on different machines, addressed correctly and reliably enough, over imperfect shared media.
items.hint: Think: who, what, how reliably, over what.

items.front: Why is a host address (e.g. an IP) not sufficient to deliver data to the right program?
items.back: A host runs many processes at once. You also need a port number so the transport layer can demultiplex to the correct process.

items.front: What is the purpose of framing on a shared medium?
items.back: It marks where one message begins and ends so a receiver can separate back-to-back transmissions from a continuous bit stream.

items.front: Why split networking into layers at all?
items.back: Separation of concerns: each layer solves one slice of the problem and trusts the layer below, so any layer can change independently of the others.
items.hint: Same reason you modularize any system.

items.front: Name three reasons real media force error handling.
items.back: Attenuation/noise flipping bits, collisions on shared media, and corruption in transit — all mean delivery is best-effort, not guaranteed.

items.prompt: Which concern does a PORT number address?
choices: Finding the physical route across the Internet
Selecting which process on a host should receive the data
Detecting bit errors on the wire
Marking the boundary between two frames
items.explanation: Host addresses (IP) locate the machine; ports demultiplex among the many processes running on that machine.

items.prompt: "Reliably enough" in the networking problem statement implies that:
choices: Every layer must guarantee perfect delivery
Reliability is optional and layered on only where the workload needs it
The physical layer corrects all errors
Reliability and addressing are the same concern
items.explanation: The base service is best-effort. Reliability (ordering, retransmission) is added at a higher layer only when an application needs it — which is exactly why UDP can skip it.

items.prompt: Why does the text call networking "the same physics in new clothes" for someone from a radar/signaling background?
choices: Because routing tables are identical to radar scopes
Because framing, clock recovery, channel contention, and SNR/link budgets are shared underlying concepts
Because both use the OSI model internally
Because IP addresses derive from pulse repetition frequency
items.explanation: The low-level signaling and contention problems are common to both fields; networking adds a protocol vocabulary on top of the same physics.

lessons.title: Two Models: OSI vs TCP/IP and Why They Disagree
lessons.content:
There are two layering models you must hold at once. The **OSI 7-layer model** is a *teaching taxonomy*: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application (mnemonic, bottom-up: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away). The **TCP/IP 4-layer model** is what actually runs: Link, Internet, Transport, Application.

They do not line up cleanly, and that mismatch is the point. TCP/IP's **Link** layer rolls OSI's Physical + Data Link together. Its **Internet** layer maps to OSI's Network. **Transport** maps directly. The interesting collapse is at the top: OSI's **Session** (5) and **Presentation** (6) layers never materialized as distinct network layers — they evaporated into applications and libraries. Session management, encryption, and data formatting are done inside the app or a library (TLS, JSON, gzip), not by a separate on-the-wire layer. So TCP/IP folds OSI layers 5–7 into one **Application** layer.

This is why the classic argument that "TLS is really presentation-layer" is both true and useless in practice: by OSI's intent TLS does presentation-layer work (it transforms the byte stream into ciphertext), but in the running stack it lives in the application layer as a library above TCP.

**Each layer has a unit — a Protocol Data Unit (PDU).** At the Link layer the PDU is a **frame**; at the Internet/Network layer it is a **packet**; at the Transport layer it is a **segment** (TCP) or **datagram** (UDP); above that you have application **data** or **messages**. Naming the PDU tells you instantly which layer someone is discussing. The takeaway: learn OSI to talk and reason; trust TCP/IP to describe what is on the wire.

items.front: List the OSI 7 layers bottom-up and a mnemonic.
items.back: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. Mnemonic: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away.
items.hint: Seven layers, P-D-N-T-S-P-A.

items.front: List the TCP/IP 4 layers and what each maps to in OSI.
items.back: Link (= OSI Physical + Data Link), Internet (= Network), Transport (= Transport), Application (= Session + Presentation + Application).
items.hint: Four layers; the top one swallows three OSI layers.

items.front: What happened to OSI's Session and Presentation layers in the real stack?
items.back: They evaporated into applications and libraries: session handling, encryption (TLS), and formatting (JSON/gzip) happen in the app, not as separate wire layers.

items.front: Match each PDU name to its layer: frame, packet, segment/datagram, data.
items.back: Frame = Link/Data Link (L2); Packet = Internet/Network (L3); Segment (TCP) or Datagram (UDP) = Transport (L4); Data/message = Application (L7).
items.hint: Naming the PDU pins the layer.

items.front: In one line, resolve "TLS is really presentation-layer."
items.back: By OSI intent yes (it transforms/encrypts the byte stream), but in the running stack it lives in the application layer as a library above TCP.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures why the two models disagree at the top?
choices: OSI omits the transport layer entirely
TCP/IP adds two extra layers above Application
OSI splits out Session and Presentation, but TCP/IP folds that work into Application/libraries
TCP/IP separates Physical from Data Link while OSI merges them
items.explanation: OSI's session/presentation responsibilities are handled inside apps and libraries in the real stack, so TCP/IP collapses OSI 5–7 into one Application layer.

items.prompt: A packet capture shows a unit at OSI layer 3. What is the correct PDU name?
choices: Frame
Packet
Segment
Datagram
items.explanation: Layer 3 is the Network/Internet layer; its PDU is the packet. Frame is L2; segment/datagram is L4.

items.prompt: Which mapping is INCORRECT?
choices: TCP/IP Link ↔ OSI Physical + Data Link
TCP/IP Internet ↔ OSI Network
TCP/IP Transport ↔ OSI Session
TCP/IP Application ↔ OSI Application + Presentation + Session
items.explanation: Transport maps to Transport. Session is one of the OSI layers absorbed into TCP/IP's Application layer.

items.prompt: Why prefer TCP/IP over OSI when describing what is actually on the wire?
choices: OSI has fewer layers and hides detail
TCP/IP is the model the running protocols (IP, TCP, Ethernet) were built around
OSI does not define a network layer
TCP/IP includes the session and presentation layers explicitly
items.explanation: The protocols that run the Internet were designed to the TCP/IP model; OSI remains a useful teaching/reasoning vocabulary.

lessons.title: Encapsulation, the End-to-End Principle & Mapping Protocols
lessons.content:
**Encapsulation** is the mechanism that makes layering real on the wire. As data descends the stack, each layer wraps the unit it receives in its own header (and sometimes a trailer) — envelopes inside envelopes. Application data gets a TCP header to become a **segment**; that segment gets an IP header to become a **packet**; that packet gets an Ethernet header and FCS trailer to become a **frame** put on the wire. On receipt the destination performs **decapsulation**: each layer strips its own header, reads its fields, and hands the payload up. Crucially, a layer never inspects the layers above it — to Ethernet, an entire IP packet is just opaque payload. This is exactly the nested framing you saw in radar pulse trains: a structure wrapping a structure wrapping a structure.

The **end-to-end principle** explains where intelligence belongs: functions like reliability, ordering, and encryption are best implemented at the communicating endpoints, not in the network's middle. The network's job is to forward best-effort; the hosts add guarantees. This is why TCP reliability lives on your machine, not in routers, and it is the design choice that let the Internet scale with a dumb, fast core and smart edges.

**Standards bodies** decide what the envelopes look like. The **IETF** publishes **RFCs** governing IP, TCP, DNS, QUIC, TLS — the Internet/Transport/Application layers. **IEEE 802** owns the LAN/link layer: 802.3 (Ethernet), 802.11 (WiFi), 802.1Q (VLANs). The **ITU** handles telecom/physical-layer and carrier standards.

Finally, the payoff skill: hand me a protocol and I name its layer. **ARP** sits at the L2/L3 boundary (resolves IP→MAC). **BGP** is application-layer in mechanics (runs over TCP/179) but does L3 routing. **QUIC** is transport, riding on UDP. **802.11** is link/physical. **DHCP** is application-layer, over UDP. Layer placement tells you where to look when it breaks.

items.front: Define encapsulation and decapsulation in one line each.
items.back: Encapsulation: each descending layer wraps the unit in its own header/trailer. Decapsulation: each ascending layer strips its own header and hands the payload up.
items.hint: Envelopes inside envelopes, going down then up.

items.front: Trace the headers added to application data as it descends to the wire.
items.back: App data + TCP header = segment; + IP header = packet; + Ethernet header & FCS trailer = frame on the wire.

items.front: State the end-to-end principle and one consequence.
items.back: Intelligence (reliability, ordering, encryption) belongs at the endpoints, not in the network core. Consequence: TCP reliability runs on hosts, letting the core stay dumb, fast, and scalable.

items.front: Which standards body owns: IP/TCP/QUIC, Ethernet/WiFi/VLANs, and carrier/telecom PHY?
items.back: IETF (RFCs) for IP/TCP/QUIC/DNS/TLS; IEEE 802 for Ethernet (802.3), WiFi (802.11), VLANs (802.1Q); ITU for telecom/carrier physical standards.

items.front: At what layer does ARP operate, and what does it do?
items.back: ARP sits at the L2/L3 boundary: given an IP (L3) it resolves the MAC (L2) needed to build the frame.

items.prompt: QUIC is best classified as which layer, and on what does it ride?
choices: Network layer, riding on IP directly
Transport layer, riding on UDP
Application layer, riding on TCP
Link layer, riding on Ethernet
items.explanation: QUIC is a transport protocol providing reliability, streams, and TLS 1.3, implemented in user space over UDP.

items.prompt: BGP is unusual because it:
choices: Runs directly on the physical layer
Performs L3 routing but runs as an application over TCP port 179
Is a link-layer protocol like ARP
Replaces IP entirely
items.explanation: BGP's job is network-layer routing between autonomous systems, but mechanically it is carried as an application over a TCP connection (port 179).

items.prompt: DHCP is which layer and over which transport?
choices: Application layer over UDP
Network layer over IP only
Transport layer over TCP
Link layer with no transport
items.explanation: DHCP is an application-layer service that uses UDP (the DORA exchange uses broadcast on UDP ports 67/68).

items.prompt: To an Ethernet frame, an encapsulated IP packet is:
choices: Inspected and rewritten field by field
Opaque payload it does not interpret
Discarded if it is not also Ethernet
Converted into a TCP segment
items.explanation: A layer treats the upper layer's unit as opaque payload — it reads only its own header. That isolation is what makes layering work.

items.prompt: 802.11 (WiFi) operates primarily at which OSI layer(s)?
choices: Transport and Application
Network and Transport
Physical and Data Link
Session and Presentation
items.explanation: 802.11 defines the wireless physical layer and the MAC (data link) layer — the link layer in TCP/IP terms.

lessons.title: From Bits to Signals: Baseband, Symbols, and Line Coding
lessons.content:
The physical layer answers one question: how do we put a bit on a wire (or fiber, or air)? The simplest scheme is **baseband** — the digital signal occupies the channel from DC upward, with voltage levels representing symbols directly. This is what Ethernet over copper does. **Broadband** instead modulates the bits onto a carrier (or several), sharing the medium in frequency — think cable TV (DOCSIS) or the RF you knew from radar, where the *information* rides on a carrier rather than living at baseband.

A crucial distinction is **symbol rate (baud)** vs **bit rate**. The baud rate is symbols per second; the bit rate is $\text{baud} \times \log_2(M)$ where $M$ is the number of distinguishable symbols. With two levels ($M=2$) one symbol carries one bit. **PAM-4**, used in 2.5G/5G/10G/25G-per-lane Ethernet, uses four amplitude levels ($M=4$), so each symbol carries 2 bits — doubling bit rate without doubling the Nyquist bandwidth. This is exactly the amplitude-discrimination tradeoff you knew from analog: more levels packed into the same swing means smaller noise margins.

**Line coding** is the choice of how symbols represent bits over time, and it exists for three engineering reasons, not aesthetics:

- **Clock recovery**: the receiver has no separate clock wire; it extracts timing from signal transitions. Long runs of identical bits with no edges starve the recovered clock and let it drift.
- **DC balance**: transformers and AC-coupled, capacitively-isolated front ends cannot pass DC. A code that averages to zero (equal 1s and 0s over a window) survives that coupling.
- **Run-length limits**: bounding the longest stretch without a transition guarantees the PLL stays locked.

Everything in this module is your home turf re-skinned: eye diagrams, jitter, and noise margins are the lens.

items.front: Baseband vs broadband signaling — what is the core difference?
items.back: Baseband puts the digital signal directly onto the medium starting at DC (e.g., Ethernet over copper, voltage levels = symbols). Broadband modulates the bits onto one or more carriers and shares the medium in frequency (e.g., DOCSIS cable, RF links). Baseband uses the whole low-frequency band for one signal; broadband multiplexes several signals in frequency.
items.hint: Where in the frequency spectrum does the information live?

items.front: How are symbol rate (baud) and bit rate related?
items.back: bit rate = baud x log2(M), where M is the number of distinguishable symbols. With M=2 (binary), 1 symbol = 1 bit so baud equals bit rate. With PAM-4 (M=4), each symbol carries 2 bits, so the bit rate is twice the baud for the same Nyquist bandwidth.
items.hint: Bits per symbol = log2(number of symbol levels).

items.front: Name the three engineering reasons line coding exists.
items.back: 1) Clock recovery — the receiver derives its bit clock from signal transitions, so the code must guarantee edges. 2) DC balance — AC-coupled/transformer-isolated front ends cannot pass DC, so the code should average to zero. 3) Run-length limits — bounding the longest run without a transition keeps the PLL locked and limits baseline wander.
items.hint: Think timing, AC coupling, and worst-case identical-bit runs.

items.front: Why does modern Ethernet (e.g., per-lane 10G/25G) use PAM-4 instead of binary?
items.back: PAM-4 uses 4 amplitude levels (M=4), carrying 2 bits per symbol. This doubles the bit rate without doubling the symbol rate / Nyquist bandwidth, which matters when the channel's usable bandwidth is the limiting factor. The cost is shrunken noise margins (3 eyes instead of 1), demanding better SNR, FEC, and DSP equalization.
items.hint: More levels = more bits/symbol, but smaller voltage margins.

items.prompt: A link runs at 5 Gbaud using PAM-4 signaling. What is its raw bit rate (before coding overhead)?
choices: 5 Gbit/s
10 Gbit/s
2.5 Gbit/s
20 Gbit/s
items.explanation: PAM-4 has M=4 symbols, so log2(4) = 2 bits per symbol. Bit rate = 5 Gbaud x 2 = 10 Gbit/s. PAM-4 is precisely the trick that lets you double the data rate within the same symbol-rate / bandwidth envelope.

items.prompt: A receiver recovers its bit clock from the incoming data stream itself (no separate clock line). Which transmitted pattern most threatens this clock recovery under plain NRZ?
choices: A rapidly alternating 0101010101 pattern
A long run of identical bits, e.g., 0000000000
A random mix of roughly equal 1s and 0s
A short 2-bit burst followed by idle
items.explanation: Under NRZ the level holds constant across each bit cell, so a long run of identical bits produces no transitions. With no edges, the receiver's PLL has nothing to lock onto and drifts, eventually sampling at the wrong instant. This is exactly why self-clocking or run-length-limited codes (Manchester, 8B/10B, scramblers) exist.

items.prompt: Why does a line code aim for DC balance (equal average of high and low over a window)?
choices: It increases the maximum achievable symbol rate
Transformer-coupled / AC-coupled receiver front ends cannot pass DC, so a nonzero average causes baseline wander and bit errors
It reduces the number of bits per symbol needed
It is required to compute the CRC at the data link layer
items.explanation: Magnetics (Ethernet transformers) and capacitive AC coupling block DC. A code with a nonzero running average accumulates a drifting baseline, shrinking the eye and causing errors. DC-balanced codes (Manchester is inherently balanced; 8B/10B uses running disparity) keep the average near zero so AC coupling works.

items.front: What does 'self-clocking' mean for a line code, and why is it valuable?
items.back: A self-clocking code guarantees enough signal transitions that the receiver can recover the bit clock from the data alone, with no separate clock wire. Manchester is the classic example (a guaranteed mid-cell transition every bit). It's valuable because a separate clock line would suffer skew over distance, and recovering timing from the data keeps transmitter and receiver in sync.
items.hint: No clock wire — extract timing from the edges in the data.

items.front: From an analog/eye-diagram standpoint, what is the tradeoff of adding more symbol levels (e.g., PAM-2 to PAM-4 to PAM-16)?
items.back: More levels pack more bits per symbol into the same symbol rate/bandwidth, but they subdivide the available voltage swing into smaller steps. That shrinks the vertical eye opening and noise margin, so each step up demands higher SNR (and usually FEC + DSP equalization). It is the classic capacity-vs-noise-margin tradeoff visible directly in the eye diagram.
items.hint: Same swing, more levels = thinner eyes.

lessons.title: The Encoding Zoo and the Shannon-Hartley Limit
lessons.content:
Real systems chain specific line codes, each solving a problem the previous one left open.

- **NRZ** (Non-Return-to-Zero): level = bit value. Simplest, most bandwidth-efficient, but no guaranteed transitions — fails clock recovery and DC balance on long runs.
- **NRZI** (NRZ-Inverted): a transition encodes a 1, no transition encodes a 0 (or vice versa). Differential, so polarity-independent, but a long run of 0s still has no edges — hence it's paired with a run-length-limiting code.
- **Manchester** (10BASE-T): each bit has a guaranteed mid-cell transition — inherently self-clocking and DC-balanced. The cost is a transition rate up to twice the bit rate, so it needs roughly double the bandwidth.
- **4B/5B** (100BASE-TX): maps every 4 data bits to a 5-bit symbol chosen so no symbol has more than three consecutive zeros — bounding run length cheaply (25% overhead). 100BASE-TX then sends these via MLT-3 to cut the spectrum.
- **8B/10B** (1G Ethernet, PCIe, SATA, Fibre Channel): maps 8 bits to 10, maintaining **running disparity** for tight DC balance and guaranteed transitions, at 25% overhead.
- **64B/66B** (10G+ Ethernet): replaces the 25% table overhead with a 2-bit sync header plus a self-synchronous scrambler — only ~3% overhead, the right choice at 10 Gbit/s and beyond.
- **PAM-4** (modern high-speed lanes): multilevel amplitude, paired with FEC.

Why stop scaling levels and rates? The **Shannon-Hartley theorem** sets the ceiling:

$$C = B\,\log_2\!\left(1 + \mathrm{SNR}\right)$$

where $C$ is capacity in bit/s, $B$ is bandwidth in Hz, and $\mathrm{SNR}$ is the linear (not dB) signal-to-noise ratio. This is the same noise-limited ceiling you reasoned about for radar detection: you buy capacity with bandwidth linearly, but with SNR only logarithmically. Doubling bandwidth doubles capacity; getting another bit per symbol requires roughly 6 dB more SNR. Every real link runs below $C$ because of coding overhead, implementation loss, and margin.

items.front: NRZ vs NRZI — how do they differ in encoding a bit?
items.back: NRZ: the signal LEVEL represents the bit value (high = 1, low = 0). NRZI: a TRANSITION (change of level) represents one bit value (commonly a 1), and no transition represents the other (a 0). NRZI is differential, so it tolerates polarity inversion, but a long run of the no-transition bit still produces no edges — so it's paired with a run-length-limiting code like 4B/5B.
items.hint: Level-based vs transition-based.

items.front: What does Manchester encoding guarantee, and what does it cost?
items.back: It guarantees a transition in the middle of every bit cell, making it inherently self-clocking and DC-balanced (each bit is half-high, half-low). The cost is bandwidth: the transition rate is up to twice the bit rate, so Manchester needs roughly double the channel bandwidth of NRZ. That's why it suited 10 Mbit/s Ethernet but was abandoned at higher rates.
items.hint: Great timing/DC properties; expensive in bandwidth.

items.front: What problem does 8B/10B solve and how, and what is its overhead?
items.back: 8B/10B (1G Ethernet, PCIe gen1/2, SATA, Fibre Channel) maps each 8-bit byte to a 10-bit symbol chosen to bound run length and maintain DC balance via 'running disparity' (it tracks the cumulative 1s-vs-0s imbalance and picks symbol variants to cancel it). Overhead is 25% (10 bits sent per 8 data bits). It also provides spare control symbols (e.g., comma/K-codes) for framing.
items.hint: 8->10 bits, running disparity, 25% overhead.

items.front: Why did 10G Ethernet switch from 8B/10B to 64B/66B?
items.back: 8B/10B's 25% overhead is expensive at 10 Gbit/s. 64B/66B prepends a 2-bit sync header to each 64-bit block and uses a self-synchronous scrambler to provide transitions and statistical DC balance, cutting overhead to about 3% (66/64). The scrambler replaces the lookup-table approach; the 2-bit header marks data vs control blocks and provides block alignment.
items.hint: Scrambler + 2-bit sync header, ~3% vs 25%.

items.prompt: A channel has bandwidth B = 20 MHz and a linear SNR of 1023 (about 30 dB). Using C = B*log2(1 + SNR), what is the approximate Shannon capacity?
choices: About 60 Mbit/s
About 200 Mbit/s
About 20 Mbit/s
About 600 Mbit/s
items.explanation: 1 + SNR = 1024 = 2^10, so log2(1024) = 10. C = 20 MHz x 10 = 200 Mbit/s. Note SNR must be linear, not dB: 30 dB = 10^(30/10) = 1000 ~ 1023. This is the noise-limited ceiling; a real 802.11 link in 20 MHz achieves less due to coding overhead, guard intervals, and margin.

items.prompt: Per Shannon-Hartley, you want to increase a link's capacity. Which statement is correct?
choices: Doubling bandwidth and doubling SNR raise capacity by the same amount
Capacity scales linearly with bandwidth but only logarithmically with SNR, so beyond a point more bandwidth buys far more capacity than more power
Capacity scales logarithmically with bandwidth and linearly with SNR
Capacity is independent of bandwidth once SNR exceeds 0 dB
items.explanation: In C = B*log2(1+SNR), B is a linear multiplier while SNR sits inside a log. Doubling B doubles C; gaining one more bit/symbol of capacity needs roughly a 4x (about 6 dB) SNR increase. This is why modern high-rate links chase bandwidth (wider channels, more lanes/fiber) rather than brute-forcing transmit power.

items.prompt: An engineer measures SNR = 25 dB and plugs 25 directly into C = B*log2(1 + SNR). What is the error?
choices: None — dB is the correct unit for the Shannon formula
SNR in the formula must be a linear ratio; 25 dB is about 316, not 25, so capacity is badly underestimated
The formula needs SNR in dB but should use log10, not log2
Bandwidth must also be converted to dB for consistency
items.explanation: The SNR in Shannon-Hartley is a linear power ratio, not decibels. 25 dB = 10^(25/10) ~ 316. Using 25 instead of 316 gives log2(26) ~ 4.7 vs log2(317) ~ 8.3 — nearly half the true capacity. Mixing dB and linear is a classic, and costly, unit error.
items.hint: dB to linear: 10^(dB/10) for a power ratio.

items.front: What does 4B/5B encoding accomplish in 100BASE-TX?
items.back: It maps each 4-bit nibble to a 5-bit code word, choosing only those 5-bit patterns with no more than three consecutive zeros (and limited leading/trailing zeros). This bounds run length so the receiver clock stays locked, at 25% overhead. 100BASE-TX then transmits the 5-bit stream using MLT-3, a 3-level code that concentrates spectral energy at a lower frequency to fit Cat5 copper.
items.hint: 4->5 bits to limit consecutive zeros; paired with MLT-3.

items.front: Connect Shannon-Hartley to your radar/analog noise intuition: what is it really saying?
items.back: It says a channel's error-free capacity is fundamentally capped by its bandwidth and SNR — the same noise floor that set your radar detection threshold. You can trade bandwidth for SNR and vice versa, but you cannot beat the log2(1+SNR) bits-per-Hz ceiling. More transmit power yields diminishing returns (log), while more bandwidth pays off linearly. Coding gets you close to C; it never exceeds it.
items.hint: The same noise-limited detection ceiling, expressed as bits/Hz.

lessons.title: Copper, Fiber, and Bringing the Link Up
lessons.content:
**Copper / twisted pair.** Ethernet over copper rides **differential signaling**: each pair carries equal-and-opposite voltages, so the receiver reads the difference. Interference couples almost equally onto both wires (common mode) and cancels in the subtraction — **common-mode rejection**, the same trick you used to pull signals out of noise on instrumentation lines. Twisting sets and stabilizes the **characteristic impedance at 100 ohm**; impedance discontinuities cause reflections, exactly like a mismatched feedline. Cat5e/6/6a/7/8 differ mainly in tested bandwidth and shielding: Cat5e (100 MHz, 1G), Cat6 (250 MHz), Cat6a (500 MHz, 10G to 100m), Cat8 (2 GHz, 25/40G short runs). Crosstalk is energy leaking between pairs: **NEXT** (Near-End) is measured at the transmitting end, **FEXT** (Far-End) at the far end. The **100 m limit** is set by attenuation and round-trip timing budgets, not an arbitrary rule.

**Fiber.** **Multimode (MMF)** has a wide core (50/62.5 um) so many propagation modes travel slightly different path lengths — **modal dispersion** spreads the pulse and limits distance; used short-reach at **850 nm** (and 1310 nm). **Single-mode (SMF)** has a ~9 um core supporting one mode, eliminating modal dispersion; used at **1310 nm** and **1550 nm** for long reach, limited mainly by **chromatic dispersion** and attenuation (1550 nm has the lowest loss, hence long-haul). Pluggable transceivers — **SFP/SFP+/QSFP** — let you match optics to the link and compute an attenuation/power budget.

**Bringing the link up.** **Auto-negotiation** exchanges capabilities (speed, duplex) via link pulses before data flows; a mismatch causes the classic duplex-mismatch slowdown. **PoE** (802.3af/at/bt) delivers up to ~90 W over the same pairs. Finally, **BER** (bit error rate) quantifies the PHY's quality — driven by SNR, dispersion, crosstalk, and reflections — and is what FEC and good cabling exist to keep low.

items.front: Why does twisted-pair Ethernet use differential signaling, and how does it reject noise?
items.back: Each pair carries equal-and-opposite (differential) voltages; the receiver looks only at the difference between the two wires. External interference (EMI) couples nearly equally onto both wires as a common-mode signal, which cancels when the receiver subtracts. This common-mode rejection lets the link survive noisy environments. The twisting keeps the two wires equally exposed so the coupling stays balanced.
items.hint: Equal-and-opposite signal; interference is common-mode and cancels.

items.front: Why is twisted-pair characteristic impedance specified at 100 ohm, and what happens on a mismatch?
items.back: A controlled, uniform 100 ohm impedance (set by conductor geometry and the twist) ensures the source, cable, and receiver are matched so signal energy transfers without reflection. Any discontinuity — a kink, bad connector, or wrong cable — creates an impedance mismatch that reflects part of the signal back, causing return loss, intersymbol interference, and bit errors. Same principle as matching a radar feedline.
items.hint: Matched impedance = no reflections; mismatch = reflections/ISI.

items.front: Distinguish NEXT from FEXT.
items.back: Both are crosstalk — unwanted coupling of a signal from one pair into another. NEXT (Near-End Crosstalk) is measured at the same end as the disturbing transmitter (the interferer's strong outgoing signal leaking into your weak incoming receive pair). FEXT (Far-End Crosstalk) is measured at the opposite end from the transmitter. NEXT is usually the bigger problem because the interfering signal is still strong at the near end.
items.hint: Measured at the transmitter end (near) vs the far end.

items.front: Why is the twisted-pair Ethernet segment limited to 100 m?
items.back: It's a budget, not an arbitrary number: signal attenuation over the cable plus connector losses must keep SNR high enough for a low BER, and legacy timing/round-trip constraints (and the standardized 90 m permanent link + 10 m patch model) bound the length. Beyond ~100 m attenuation and accumulated noise push BER too high. Fiber or repeaters/switches extend reach.
items.hint: Attenuation + timing budget; 90 m link + 10 m patch.

items.prompt: Why does single-mode fiber (SMF) reach much farther than multimode fiber (MMF)?
choices: SMF carries more optical power because its core is larger
SMF's ~9 um core supports essentially one propagation mode, eliminating modal dispersion that spreads pulses in MMF's wide core
SMF uses 850 nm light, which attenuates less than MMF's 1550 nm
MMF cannot be used with laser sources, only LEDs, which limits its range
items.explanation: MMF's wide core (50/62.5 um) lets many modes travel slightly different path lengths, so a pulse smears out (modal dispersion), limiting distance. SMF's ~9 um core supports a single mode, removing modal dispersion; its reach is then limited mainly by chromatic dispersion and attenuation. SMF typically runs at 1310/1550 nm (1550 nm has the lowest loss); 850 nm is the short-reach MMF wavelength.

items.prompt: For the longest-haul single-mode fiber links, which wavelength is preferred and why?
choices: 850 nm, because short wavelengths attenuate least
1310 nm, because it has zero chromatic dispersion and the lowest loss
1550 nm, because silica fiber has its lowest attenuation there (enabling EDFA amplification)
650 nm visible light, because it is easiest to align
items.explanation: Silica fiber's attenuation minimum is near 1550 nm, so long-haul/DWDM links use it; it also sits in the band where erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs) work. 1310 nm is the low/zero chromatic-dispersion window but has higher loss; 850 nm is short-reach multimode. Lower loss means longer spans between amplifiers.

items.prompt: Auto-negotiation is disabled on one switch port and hard-set to full duplex, while the other end auto-negotiates. What is the classic symptom?
choices: The link fails to come up at all
A duplex mismatch: the auto end falls back to half duplex, causing late collisions, CRC errors, and severe throughput loss under load
The link runs at full speed with no issues
Both ends silently negotiate 10 Mbit/s and stay there permanently
items.explanation: When one end can't negotiate duplex, the auto-negotiating end defaults to half duplex while the forced end runs full duplex. The full-duplex side transmits whenever it wants; the half-duplex side sees that as collisions, producing late collisions, FCS/CRC errors, and throughput that collapses under bidirectional load. The link still passes small pings, which makes it a sneaky bug.

items.front: What does Power over Ethernet (PoE) do, and roughly how much power do the standards deliver?
items.back: PoE delivers DC power to a device (AP, IP camera, phone) over the same twisted pairs that carry data, removing the need for a separate power adapter. 802.3af (PoE) provides ~15.4 W at the source (~12.95 W at the device), 802.3at (PoE+) ~30 W, and 802.3bt (PoE++ / 4PPoE) up to ~60 W (Type 3) or ~90 W (Type 4) using all four pairs.
items.hint: Power + data on one cable; af/at/bt tiers.

items.front: What is BER, what drives it, and how is it kept low?
items.back: BER (Bit Error Rate) is the fraction of received bits that are wrong, e.g., 10^-12 means one error per trillion bits. It's driven by SNR, attenuation, dispersion (fiber), crosstalk and reflections (copper), and jitter. It's kept low by good cabling/impedance matching, adequate link margin, equalization/DSP, and forward error correction (FEC). It's the PHY's bottom-line quality metric and ties directly to the eye-diagram opening.
items.hint: Errors per bit; SNR/dispersion/crosstalk drive it; FEC fights it.

items.front: Study this waveform: the byte 0b10110010 under NRZ vs Manchester. What two properties does Manchester guarantee that NRZ does not, and at what cost?
items.back: Manchester puts a guaranteed transition in the middle of every bit cell, giving it (1) self-clocking (the receiver recovers its clock from those edges, even on long runs where NRZ goes flat) and (2) DC balance (each cell is half-high/half-low, so the average is zero and AC coupling works). The cost is bandwidth: the transition rate is up to twice the bit rate. NRZ is bandwidth-efficient but has no edges during long identical-bit runs.

subtopics.title: Data Link: Ethernet, Framing, MAC & Switching

lessons.title: The Data Link Layer: MAC, LLC & the 48-bit Address
lessons.content:
The **data link layer** (OSI layer 2) takes the raw bit-pipe the physical layer offers and turns it into something useful: addressed, delimited **frames** delivered across a single link or LAN segment. IEEE 802 splits it into two sublayers. The lower **MAC (Media Access Control)** sublayer owns the hardware address, the frame format, and the rules for who may transmit on a shared medium. The upper **LLC (Logical Link Control, IEEE 802.2)** sublayer was meant to multiplex upper-layer protocols and offer optional reliability. In practice, modern Ethernet (Ethernet II / DIX) skips LLC entirely and identifies the upper protocol with the EtherType field, so you will rarely meet 802.2 LLC outside legacy or 802.11 contexts.

The **MAC address** is a 48-bit (6-byte) identifier written as six hex octets, e.g. `00:1B:44:11:3A:B7`. It is structured, not random. The top three bytes are the **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)**, assigned by the IEEE to a vendor; the lower three bytes are assigned by that vendor per-NIC. Two bits in the very first byte carry meaning. The least-significant bit of the first octet is the **I/G (individual/group) bit**: 0 means unicast, 1 means multicast (group). The next bit is the **U/L (universal/local) bit**: 0 means a globally-unique burned-in address, 1 means a **locally-administered address (LAA)** set by software. The all-ones address `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` is the L2 **broadcast**. As an ex-radar tech you can read this exactly like a structured tag field: a vendor prefix plus a per-unit serial, with two flag bits riding in the header.

items.front: What are the two sublayers of the IEEE 802 data link layer, and what does each do?
items.back: MAC (Media Access Control): hardware addressing, frame format, and medium-access rules. LLC (Logical Link Control, 802.2): multiplexes upper-layer protocols and offers optional flow/error control. Modern Ethernet II uses the EtherType field instead of LLC.
items.hint: Lower handles the wire and the address; upper handles protocol multiplexing.

items.front: How many bits is a MAC address, and how is it split structurally?
items.back: 48 bits / 6 octets. The upper 3 bytes are the OUI (vendor prefix assigned by IEEE); the lower 3 bytes are assigned by the vendor per device.
items.hint: Vendor prefix + per-unit serial.

items.front: In a MAC address, what do the I/G bit and the U/L bit indicate, and where are they?
items.back: Both live in the first octet. The least-significant bit is I/G: 0 = unicast (individual), 1 = multicast (group). The next bit is U/L: 0 = universal/globally-unique (burned-in), 1 = locally administered (software-set).
items.hint: Two lowest-order bits of the very first byte.

items.front: What is the Ethernet broadcast MAC address?
items.back: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (all 48 bits set). A frame to this address is delivered to every host in the broadcast domain.
items.hint: Every bit is 1.

items.prompt: You see a frame destined for MAC address 01:00:5E:7F:00:01. Looking only at the address, what kind of frame is it?
choices: A unicast frame (the I/G bit is 0)
A multicast frame (the I/G bit is 1)
A broadcast frame (all bits set)
A locally-administered unicast frame
items.explanation: The first octet is 0x01 = 0000 0001 in binary. The least-significant bit (I/G) is 1, so it is multicast. (01:00:5E is in fact the IANA OUI used for mapping IPv4 multicast groups onto Ethernet.) It is not broadcast because not all bits are set.

items.prompt: A virtual machine's NIC has the MAC 02:42:AC:11:00:02. What does the first octet tell you?
choices: It is a multicast address
It is the burned-in address from a hardware vendor's OUI
It is a unicast, locally-administered address
It is an invalid MAC address
items.explanation: 0x02 = 0000 0010. The I/G bit (lowest) is 0 -> unicast. The U/L bit (next) is 1 -> locally administered, i.e. set by software rather than a vendor OUI. Hypervisors and containers (e.g. Docker's 02:42 prefix) commonly use LAAs.

items.prompt: Why does modern Ethernet II rarely use the 802.2 LLC sublayer?
choices: LLC is illegal under current IEEE standards
The EtherType field already identifies the upper-layer protocol, making LLC's multiplexing redundant
LLC only works on fiber, not copper
Switches strip the LLC header before forwarding
items.explanation: Ethernet II carries a 2-byte EtherType that names the upper protocol directly (e.g. 0x0800 = IPv4). That makes the LLC/SNAP multiplexing machinery unnecessary, so LLC survives mainly in legacy 802.3 and in 802.11.

items.front: What is a locally-administered address (LAA) and where do you commonly see them?
items.back: A MAC with the U/L bit set to 1, meaning it was assigned by software rather than burned in from a vendor OUI. Common on VMs, containers (Docker uses 02:42:...), and bonded/failover interfaces.
items.hint: U/L bit = 1; think hypervisors and containers.

lessons.title: The Ethernet II Frame, Byte by Byte
lessons.content:
Lay an Ethernet II frame out left to right and every byte has a job. First on the wire come **7 bytes of preamble** (alternating `10101010`) followed by a **1-byte SFD (Start Frame Delimiter, `10101011`)**. These eight bytes let the receiver's clock-recovery circuit lock onto the bit timing and find the frame boundary; they are a physical-layer artifact and are **not** counted as part of the frame and **not** covered by the FCS.

The frame proper begins with the **6-byte destination MAC**, then the **6-byte source MAC**, then a **2-byte EtherType** identifying the payload protocol (0x0800 IPv4, 0x86DD IPv6, 0x0806 ARP, 0x8100 a VLAN tag). Next is the **payload**, **46 to 1500 bytes**. The 1500-byte ceiling is the standard **MTU**; if the upper layer has fewer than 46 bytes it is **padded** up to 46. Finally a **4-byte FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** holds a **CRC-32** over the destination, source, type, and payload fields.

Do the arithmetic: 6 + 6 + 2 + 46 + 4 = **64 bytes minimum**, and 6 + 6 + 2 + 1500 + 4 = **1518 bytes maximum** (1522 with a VLAN tag). **Jumbo frames** raise the payload to roughly 9000 bytes to cut per-frame overhead on storage and data-center links; they are non-standard, so every device in the path must agree or the frames are dropped. The CRC-32 is a powerful **error-detection** code — it catches essentially all real-world bit errors — but it is purely a checksum: Ethernet **detects and discards** corrupt frames, it never corrects them. Recovery is left to a higher layer (TCP) or simply dropped (UDP).

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

items.front: List the fields of an Ethernet II frame in order, with their sizes.
items.back: Preamble (7 B) + SFD (1 B) [PHY sync, not part of frame] | Destination MAC (6 B) | Source MAC (6 B) | EtherType (2 B) | Payload (46-1500 B) | FCS/CRC-32 (4 B).
items.hint: Sync, then dst, src, type, data, check.

items.front: What is the minimum and maximum size of a standard (untagged) Ethernet frame, and why?
items.back: Minimum 64 bytes (6+6+2+46+4), maximum 1518 bytes (6+6+2+1500+4). The 1500-byte payload is the standard MTU; payloads under 46 bytes are padded. Preamble/SFD are not counted.
items.hint: Sum the header/trailer plus the 46-1500 payload range.

items.front: What does the EtherType field do, and give three common values.
items.back: It identifies the upper-layer protocol carried in the payload. 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x86DD = IPv6, 0x0806 = ARP, 0x8100 = 802.1Q VLAN tag. Values >= 0x0600 are EtherTypes (smaller values were 802.3 length).
items.hint: Two bytes after the source MAC; >= 0x0600.

items.front: What is a jumbo frame and what is the catch?
items.back: A frame with a payload up to ~9000 bytes (vs the standard 1500), used to reduce per-frame overhead on data-center/storage links. It is non-standard, so every NIC, switch, and router along the path must be configured for the same large MTU or the frames are dropped/fragmented.
items.hint: ~9000-byte payload; end-to-end agreement required.

items.prompt: An IP packet hands only 20 bytes of payload to Ethernet. What happens?
choices: The frame is sent with a 20-byte payload
The frame is rejected as too small
The payload is padded with zeros up to 46 bytes
The preamble is shortened to compensate
items.explanation: The minimum payload is 46 bytes, so a short payload is padded to 46. This keeps the total frame at least 64 bytes, a requirement that dates back to collision-detection timing on shared media.

items.prompt: A frame arrives and its computed CRC-32 does not match the FCS field. What does the Ethernet hardware do?
choices: Corrects the error using the CRC and forwards the frame
Discards the frame; any recovery is left to upper layers
Requests a retransmission from the sender at layer 2
Strips the FCS and forwards the frame anyway
items.explanation: CRC-32 is error-DETECTION only. A mismatch means corruption, so the frame is silently dropped (and counted as an error). Ethernet has no L2 retransmission for this; recovery, if any, is TCP's job, while UDP/real-time traffic just loses the data.

items.prompt: Which of these is NOT counted in the Ethernet frame size or covered by the FCS?
choices: The destination MAC address
The EtherType field
The preamble and SFD
The payload
items.explanation: The 7-byte preamble and 1-byte SFD are physical-layer synchronization bytes. They precede the frame on the wire, are not part of the 64-1518 byte frame size, and are not included in the CRC-32 computation.

items.front: What does the FCS contain and what does it protect?
items.back: A 4-byte CRC-32 computed over the destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType, and payload. It detects (not corrects) bit errors in those fields. Preamble/SFD are excluded.
items.hint: Cyclic redundancy check over the frame body, trailer position.

lessons.title: From CSMA/CD to Switches: How L2 Forwarding Works
lessons.content:
Original Ethernet was a **shared bus**: every host saw every frame and only one could transmit at a time. **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** governed it — listen before sending, and if two stations transmit at once, detect the collision, jam, back off a random interval, and retry. This is the **reason the minimum frame is 64 bytes**: a sender must still be transmitting when the most distant collision signal returns, or it would finish and never notice the collision. The 64-byte floor, the maximum cable length, and the 10 Mbps rate are bound together by this round-trip timing. On a modern **full-duplex switched link** there is no contention, so CSMA/CD is disabled and effectively irrelevant — but it still explains the legacy 64-byte minimum you compute today.

The device that killed CSMA/CD is the **switch**. A **hub** is a dumb multiport repeater: one collision domain, every port sees every bit. A **bridge** was the two-port ancestor of the switch; a switch is a fast, many-port bridge implemented in hardware. A switch **learns** by recording the *source* MAC of every incoming frame against the port it arrived on, building a **MAC/CAM forwarding table**. To forward, it looks up the *destination* MAC: 

- **Known unicast** -> forward out the single learned port. 
- **Unknown unicast** (not yet in the table) -> **flood** out all ports except the ingress. 
- **Broadcast** (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) -> flood everywhere. 
- **Multicast** -> flood by default, or send only to subscribed ports if IGMP snooping is enabled. 

Crucially, each switch port is its **own collision domain** (so collisions essentially vanish), but the whole switch is **one broadcast domain** until you introduce VLANs or a router.

items.front: What does CSMA/CD stand for and what problem does it solve?
items.back: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. On a shared half-duplex medium it arbitrates access: listen before sending, detect collisions, jam, back off randomly, and retry. Obsolete on full-duplex switched links.
items.hint: Listen, send, detect collision, back off.

items.front: Why is the minimum Ethernet frame 64 bytes, in terms of CSMA/CD?
items.back: A transmitting station must still be sending when the worst-case collision signal travels to the far end of the segment and back. 64 bytes at 10 Mbps over the maximum cable length guarantees the sender is still on the wire to detect the collision (the 'slot time').
items.hint: Round-trip propagation time vs transmit time.

items.front: How does a switch learn where hosts are?
items.back: It reads the SOURCE MAC of each incoming frame and records (source MAC -> ingress port, with an aging timer) in its MAC/CAM forwarding table. Forwarding decisions then use the DESTINATION MAC lookup.
items.hint: Learn from source, forward by destination.

items.prompt: Trace the switch behavior for each frame type. A switch receives frames on port 3. Which row is correct?
choices: Unknown unicast -> drop; Known unicast -> flood; Broadcast -> single port; Multicast -> drop
Unknown unicast -> flood all but port 3; Known unicast -> forward only to the learned port; Broadcast -> flood all but port 3; Multicast -> flood (or subscribed ports with IGMP snooping)
Unknown unicast -> forward to learned port; Known unicast -> flood; Broadcast -> drop; Multicast -> flood
All four types -> flood out every port including port 3
items.explanation: This is the canonical L2 forwarding behavior. Unknown unicast (dst not in CAM) is flooded out all ports except ingress; known unicast goes only to the learned port; broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) floods to all but ingress; multicast floods by default unless IGMP snooping restricts it to subscribers. A switch never forwards a frame back out the port it came in on.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between a hub and a switch regarding collision domains?
choices: A hub gives each port its own collision domain; a switch shares one
Both put all ports in one collision domain
A hub is one big collision domain; a switch makes each port its own collision domain
Neither has collision domains because Ethernet is full-duplex
items.explanation: A hub is a dumb repeater: every port shares a single collision domain, so traffic from any host can collide with any other. A switch isolates each port into its own collision domain (and runs full-duplex), which is why collisions essentially disappear on switched networks.

items.prompt: A switch with no VLANs configured forms how many broadcast domains and how many collision domains for its 24 ports?
choices: 24 broadcast domains, 1 collision domain
1 broadcast domain, 24 collision domains
1 broadcast domain, 1 collision domain
24 broadcast domains, 24 collision domains
items.explanation: Each switch port is its own collision domain (24 of them). But absent VLANs or a router, the entire switch is a single broadcast domain: a broadcast frame floods out all ports. Segmenting the broadcast domain requires VLANs (L2) or routing (L3).

items.front: Hub vs bridge vs switch: one line each.
items.back: Hub = dumb multiport repeater, one collision domain, no addressing. Bridge = early 2-port device that learns MACs and separates segments (software). Switch = a fast, many-port hardware bridge that learns MACs and forwards per-port.
items.hint: Repeater, ancestor, modern hardware version.

items.front: Why is CSMA/CD irrelevant on a modern switched link, yet still worth knowing?
items.back: Full-duplex switched links have separate Tx/Rx paths and no shared medium, so collisions cannot occur and CSMA/CD is disabled. It still matters because it explains the legacy 64-byte minimum frame size and the historical cable-length/rate constraints.
items.hint: Full duplex = no contention; but it explains the 64-byte floor.

lessons.title: VLANs, 802.1Q, Spanning Tree, LACP & Flow Control
lessons.content:
A single broadcast domain does not scale, so we segment one physical switch into many logical LANs with **VLANs (Virtual LANs)**. Hosts in different VLANs cannot reach each other at L2; traffic must cross a **router or L3 switch**, which is also where you enforce policy and security. To carry multiple VLANs over one **trunk** link, **IEEE 802.1Q** inserts a **4-byte tag** between the source MAC and EtherType. It holds a 2-byte TPID (0x8100, which appears as the EtherType), a **3-bit PCP (Priority Code Point)** for class-of-service, a 1-bit DEI, and a **12-bit VLAN ID** (4094 usable VLANs). A tagged frame's max size grows to 1522 bytes.

Redundant links create **loops**, and an Ethernet frame has **no TTL** — nothing decrements and discards a circulating frame the way IP's TTL does. A broadcast caught in a loop is re-flooded forever, a **broadcast storm** that saturates links and melts the network in seconds, with MAC tables thrashing as the same source appears on multiple ports. **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP, 802.1D)** prevents this by electing a root bridge and logically **blocking** redundant ports to leave one loop-free active tree, re-converging if a link fails. Classic STP takes ~30-50 s; **RSTP (802.1w)** converges in well under a second and is the modern default.

When you *want* to use parallel links for bandwidth and redundancy without a loop, bundle them with **LACP (802.3ad Link Aggregation)** into one logical link; the bundle load-balances flows and survives individual link failures. Finally, **802.3x flow control** lets a congested receiver send a **PAUSE** frame asking the sender to stop briefly - a blunt, link-local backpressure mechanism, largely superseded by higher-layer congestion control but still seen in lossless data-center fabrics.

items.front: What is a VLAN, and how do hosts in different VLANs communicate?
items.back: A VLAN is a logical broadcast domain layered onto a physical switch, isolating groups of ports at L2. Hosts in different VLANs cannot talk directly; their traffic must be routed by a router or L3 switch (inter-VLAN routing).
items.hint: Logical LAN segmentation; crossing them needs L3.

items.front: Describe the 802.1Q tag: where it sits and its fields.
items.back: A 4-byte tag inserted between source MAC and EtherType. Fields: 16-bit TPID (0x8100), 3-bit PCP (priority/CoS), 1-bit DEI, 12-bit VLAN ID (4094 usable). Tagged frames can be up to 1522 bytes.
items.hint: TPID + PCP + DEI + 12-bit VID, after src MAC.

items.prompt: Why is a Layer 2 loop catastrophic in a way that a Layer 3 routing loop is not?
choices: L2 frames are larger than L3 packets so they congest faster
Ethernet frames have no TTL field, so a looping frame (especially a broadcast) circulates forever, causing a broadcast storm
Routers cannot detect loops at all
L2 loops corrupt the FCS, which L3 loops do not
items.explanation: IP packets carry a TTL that is decremented at each hop and dropped at zero, bounding routing loops. Ethernet frames have no such field, so a looping frame is never discarded. A broadcast or unknown-unicast frame gets re-flooded endlessly, a broadcast storm that saturates links and thrashes MAC tables within seconds.

items.prompt: What does Spanning Tree Protocol do to prevent loops?
choices: It adds a TTL to Ethernet frames
It elects a root bridge and logically blocks redundant ports, leaving a single loop-free active tree
It encrypts broadcast frames so they cannot loop
It routes frames using IP addresses
items.explanation: STP (802.1D) builds a loop-free topology by electing a root bridge and putting redundant ports into a blocking state, so exactly one active path exists between any two points. If a link fails it re-converges to restore connectivity. RSTP (802.1w) does the same far faster.

items.prompt: What is the main improvement of RSTP (802.1w) over classic STP (802.1D)?
choices: It supports more VLANs
It encrypts BPDUs
It converges in well under a second instead of ~30-50 seconds
It eliminates the need for a root bridge
items.explanation: RSTP's headline benefit is dramatically faster convergence (sub-second vs the ~30-50 s of classic STP), achieved with proposal/agreement handshakes and edge/point-to-point port roles. It still elects a root bridge.

items.front: What is LACP (802.3ad) and what does it provide?
items.back: Link Aggregation Control Protocol bundles multiple physical links into one logical link. It increases aggregate bandwidth, load-balances flows across members, and provides redundancy (the bundle survives loss of individual links) - all without creating an STP-blocked loop.
items.hint: Bond parallel links into one; bandwidth + redundancy.

items.front: What does an 802.3x PAUSE frame do?
items.back: It is link-local flow control: a congested receiver sends a PAUSE frame telling the directly-connected sender to stop transmitting for a specified time, applying backpressure. It is blunt and largely superseded by higher-layer congestion control, but still used in lossless data-center fabrics.
items.hint: Receiver tells the sender 'stop for a moment'.

items.prompt: How large is the VLAN ID field in an 802.1Q tag, and how many usable VLANs does that allow?
choices: 8 bits, 256 VLANs
10 bits, 1024 VLANs
12 bits, 4094 usable VLANs (0 and 4095 reserved)
16 bits, 65536 VLANs
items.explanation: The VLAN ID is 12 bits, giving 4096 values; VLAN 0 (priority-only) and 4095 (reserved) are not usable as normal VLANs, leaving 4094. This 4094 ceiling is a key motivation for VXLAN overlays in large multi-tenant data centers.

items.front: How does a trunk link differ from an access port?
items.back: An access port belongs to a single VLAN and carries untagged frames to/from an end host. A trunk port carries traffic for multiple VLANs between switches (or to a router), using 802.1Q tags to keep the VLANs distinguished.
items.hint: Access = one VLAN, untagged; trunk = many VLANs, tagged.

lessons.title: The Address Resolution Problem
lessons.content:
You have an IP address; to actually put bits on the wire you need a **MAC address**. The Ethernet frame header carries a destination MAC, not an IP. So before a host can send the first packet to a same-subnet peer, it must answer: *"Given 192.168.1.20, what is the 48-bit hardware address I should put in the frame?"* That translation is the job of **ARP** (Address Resolution Protocol, RFC 826). It is the literal glue between Layer 3 (logical IP) and Layer 2 (physical MAC).

The exchange is two messages. **ARP request**: the sender broadcasts a frame to `ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff` asking "Who has 192.168.1.20? Tell 192.168.1.10." Because it's a broadcast, every host in the broadcast domain (the switched LAN / VLAN) receives and inspects it. **ARP reply**: only the host that owns that IP responds, and it replies as a **unicast** straight back to the requester's MAC: ".20 is at bb:bb:bb:22:22:22."

Notice ARP rides directly on Ethernet (EtherType 0x0806) — it is *not* carried inside IP. It's a peer of IP, not a passenger. ARP only resolves addresses *within one subnet*; it has no concept of routing. The moment a destination is on a different subnet, the question changes entirely (covered in the next lesson). For a radar tech: think of the broadcast request as an omnidirectional interrogation pulse, and the unicast reply as the single transponder that recognizes its own code answering back on a directed beam.

items.front: What problem does ARP solve?
items.back: You have a destination IPv4 address but the Ethernet frame needs a destination MAC address. ARP resolves an IP on the local subnet to its hardware (MAC) address.
items.hint: L3 logical address -> L2 hardware address.

items.front: What destination MAC does an ARP request use, and why?
items.back: ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff (broadcast). The sender doesn't yet know the target's MAC, so it must flood the request to every host in the broadcast domain.
items.hint: All ones.

items.front: Is the ARP reply broadcast or unicast?
items.back: Unicast. The responder already learned the requester's MAC from the request frame, so it answers directly to that host instead of bothering everyone.
items.hint: It learned the asker's MAC from the request.

items.front: Does ARP run on top of IP?
items.back: No. ARP rides directly on Ethernet with its own EtherType (0x0806). It is a peer of IP, not encapsulated inside an IP packet.
items.hint: Its own EtherType, not IP protocol number.

items.front: What is the scope of ARP resolution?
items.back: A single subnet / broadcast domain. ARP cannot resolve an address that lives on another subnet; broadcasts don't cross routers.
items.hint: Routers stop broadcasts.

items.prompt: A host wants to send an IP packet to 192.168.1.20 on its own subnet but has no entry for it. What does it do first?
choices: Send the IP packet to the default gateway
Broadcast an ARP request asking who owns 192.168.1.20
Send a DNS query for the hostname
Unicast an ARP request directly to 192.168.1.20
items.explanation: It can't unicast yet because it doesn't know the MAC. It broadcasts an ARP request; only the owner of .20 replies, this time as a unicast.

items.prompt: Which field in the outgoing Ethernet frame does ARP ultimately fill in?
choices: Destination IP address
Source IP address
Destination MAC address
EtherType
items.explanation: The frame already carries the IPs inside the IP header; ARP supplies the destination MAC for the Layer-2 header so the frame can actually be delivered on the wire.

items.prompt: At which layer boundary does ARP sit?
choices: Between the application and transport layers
Between the transport and network layers
Between the network (L3) and data-link (L2) layers
Between the data-link and physical layers
items.explanation: ARP maps L3 IP addresses to L2 MAC addresses, making it the glue at the network/data-link boundary.

lessons.title: The ARP Cache & First Contact
lessons.content:
Broadcasting for every packet would be absurd, so each host keeps an **ARP cache** (the neighbor table): a short-lived map of IP -> MAC. View it with `arp -a` (legacy) or the modern Linux `ip neigh`, which also exposes states like `REACHABLE`, `STALE`, `DELAY`, and `FAILED`. Entries **age out** (typically tens of seconds to a few minutes) so the table tracks reality as hosts move, swap NICs, or change addresses. A stale or wrong entry is a classic cause of "works, then mysteriously doesn't" bugs.

The defining question is **first contact**, and the answer pivots on one decision the sending host makes: *is the destination IP on my own subnet, or not?* It computes this with its subnet mask.

**Same subnet:** Host A (192.168.1.10/24) talks to B (192.168.1.20). A finds B is local, ARPs for B's MAC, and builds a frame addressed *directly to B's MAC*. One ARP exchange, then the ICMP/data flows host-to-host.

**Across a router:** A (192.168.1.10/24) talks to 8.8.8.8. A computes that 8.8.8.8 is *not* local, so it must hand the packet to its **default gateway** (192.168.1.1). It does **not** ARP for 8.8.8.8 — that would be pointless, since the reply could never come back. Instead it ARPs for the *gateway's* MAC, then builds a frame whose **destination MAC is the router's**, but whose **destination IP is still 8.8.8.8**. The router strips the frame, makes its own forwarding decision, and re-frames toward the next hop. Key insight: the **IP addresses stay end-to-end; the MAC addresses are rewritten hop by hop.** Every router crossing triggers a fresh ARP for the next-hop MAC.

items.front: What is the ARP cache and why does it exist?
items.back: A per-host table of recently resolved IP->MAC mappings. It avoids broadcasting an ARP request for every single packet by remembering neighbors for a short time.
items.hint: Avoid re-broadcasting constantly.

items.front: Modern Linux command to view/manage the neighbor (ARP) table?
items.back: `ip neigh` (the legacy command is `arp -a`). `ip neigh` shows states such as REACHABLE, STALE, DELAY, and FAILED.
items.hint: iproute2 'neighbor' object.

items.front: Why do ARP cache entries age out?
items.back: So the table tracks reality: hosts move, NICs/MACs change, IPs get reassigned. A never-expiring entry would deliver frames to the wrong (or gone) MAC.
items.hint: Hardware and addresses change over time.

items.front: First contact, SAME subnet: what happens packet by packet?
items.back: Host computes the destination is local -> ARP-broadcasts for its MAC -> gets unicast reply -> caches it -> sends the frame with the destination's own MAC. Then the actual data (e.g., ICMP echo) flows directly.
items.hint: ARP for the destination itself.

items.front: First contact ACROSS a router: whose MAC does the sender ARP for?
items.back: The default gateway's MAC, NOT the remote host's. The frame's destination MAC is the router; the destination IP remains the far-away host. ARPing for an off-subnet IP would be useless.
items.hint: You can't ARP something a broadcast can't reach.

items.front: As a packet crosses multiple routers, what stays constant and what changes?
items.back: Source/destination IP addresses stay end-to-end. The L2 MAC addresses are rewritten at every hop (each router ARPs for the next hop). TTL also decrements per hop.
items.hint: L3 fixed, L2 per-hop.

items.prompt: Host 192.168.1.10/24 wants to ping 8.8.8.8. What does it ARP for?
choices: 8.8.8.8 directly
Its own default gateway's IP
The DNS server
Nothing; ARP is not used for off-subnet traffic at all
items.explanation: 8.8.8.8 is off-subnet, so the host sends the packet to its default gateway. It ARPs for the gateway's MAC; the frame's dst MAC is the router, but the dst IP stays 8.8.8.8.

items.prompt: On the same subnet, after A resolves B's MAC and caches it, what is in the destination MAC field of A's data frames to B?
choices: ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
The default gateway's MAC
B's own MAC address
A's own MAC address
items.explanation: Same-subnet delivery is direct: the frame carries B's actual MAC. No gateway is involved.

items.prompt: You see intermittent connectivity to a server right after its NIC was replaced. ARP-related cause?
choices: DNS TTL too high
A stale ARP cache entry still pointing at the old MAC until it ages out
The subnet mask is wrong
TCP window scaling disabled
items.explanation: Until the stale IP->old-MAC entry ages out (or a gratuitous ARP refreshes it), frames go to a MAC that no longer exists, producing intermittent failures.

lessons.title: Gratuitous ARP, Proxy ARP, Spoofing & IPv6 NDP
lessons.content:
**Gratuitous ARP (GARP)** is an unsolicited ARP announcement: a host broadcasts "this IP is at *my* MAC" without anyone asking. Uses: **IP conflict detection** (boot-time announce; if someone else replies, there's a duplicate), refreshing neighbors' caches, and **failover**. In **VRRP/HSRP**, when a backup router takes over the virtual IP, it sends a GARP so every switch and host updates to the new MAC instantly instead of waiting for cache aging — the mechanism behind near-seamless gateway failover.

**Proxy ARP** lets a router answer ARP requests *on behalf of* hosts on another segment, replying with its own MAC so a misconfigured or split host still reaches them. Handy historically; today it's mostly a foot-gun that masks real subnet design problems.

**ARP spoofing/poisoning** is the canonical L2 attack. ARP has **no authentication** — any host can send a forged reply claiming "the gateway's IP is at *my* MAC." Victims cache the lie and send their traffic to the attacker, enabling man-in-the-middle or denial of service. Tools like `arpspoof` make it trivial; defenses are Dynamic ARP Inspection, DHCP snooping, and static entries. It works precisely because ARP trusts any reply.

**IPv6 has no ARP.** Its replacement is the **Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP)**, carried inside **ICMPv6**. The four core messages: **Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement (NS/NA)** do ARP's job (using multicast, not broadcast — IPv6 has no broadcast), and **Router Solicitation/Advertisement (RS/RA)** handle gateway discovery and feed SLAAC autoconfiguration. Finally, the ancestors: **RARP** (MAC -> IP, the reverse lookup for diskless boot) and **BOOTP** evolved into **DHCP** — the modern way hosts learn their own address, gateway, and DNS.

items.front: What is a gratuitous ARP and name two uses.
items.back: An unsolicited ARP announcing 'this IP is at my MAC' with no request. Uses: IP-conflict detection at boot, and failover (e.g., VRRP/HSRP) to instantly update neighbor caches to the new MAC.
items.hint: Nobody asked, but here's my mapping.

items.front: Why does VRRP/HSRP send a gratuitous ARP during failover?
items.back: So switches and hosts immediately relearn that the virtual gateway IP now lives at the new active router's MAC, instead of waiting for stale entries to age out. Enables fast, near-seamless failover.
items.hint: Refresh everyone's cache right now.

items.front: What is proxy ARP?
items.back: A router answering ARP requests on behalf of hosts on another segment, supplying its own MAC so traffic flows through it. Mostly historical / a workaround that hides real subnetting problems.
items.hint: Router answers for someone else.

items.front: Why does ARP spoofing work so easily?
items.back: ARP has no authentication. Any host can send a forged unsolicited reply mapping the gateway's IP to the attacker's MAC; victims cache it blindly, enabling man-in-the-middle or DoS.
items.hint: No way to verify a reply is legit.

items.front: What replaces ARP in IPv6, and what protocol carries it?
items.back: The Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP), carried inside ICMPv6. It uses multicast (IPv6 has no broadcast) instead of broadcast.
items.hint: NDP over ICMPv6.

items.front: Name the four core NDP message types and their roles.
items.back: Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement (NS/NA) = address resolution (ARP's job). Router Solicitation/Advertisement (RS/RA) = gateway discovery and SLAAC autoconfiguration parameters.
items.hint: NS/NA for neighbors, RS/RA for routers.

items.front: What are RARP and BOOTP, historically?
items.back: RARP (Reverse ARP) maps a known MAC to an IP, used by diskless hosts to learn their own address. BOOTP extended that idea; both are ancestors of DHCP.
items.hint: Early ways to get your own IP -> DHCP.

items.prompt: An attacker on your LAN sends frames claiming the gateway IP 192.168.1.1 maps to the attacker's MAC. What is this, and why does it succeed?
choices: Proxy ARP; routers are allowed to answer for any IP
ARP spoofing; ARP replies are unauthenticated and cached blindly
Gratuitous ARP for conflict detection; it's benign
RARP; it reverses the IP-to-MAC mapping
items.explanation: It's ARP spoofing/poisoning. Because ARP has no authentication, victims accept the forged reply and route their traffic through the attacker (MITM).

items.prompt: Which IPv6 NDP message pair performs the same function ARP did (resolving a neighbor's link-layer address)?
choices: Router Solicitation / Router Advertisement
Echo Request / Echo Reply
Neighbor Solicitation / Neighbor Advertisement
Discover / Offer
items.explanation: NS/NA resolve an IPv6 address to a MAC, the direct analog of ARP request/reply. RS/RA handle router/gateway discovery instead.

items.prompt: Why can't IPv6 NDP use broadcast the way ARP does?
choices: IPv6 frames are too large for broadcast
IPv6 has no broadcast address; NDP uses multicast (solicited-node) instead
Broadcast is blocked by all IPv6 routers
NDP runs over TCP, which can't broadcast
items.explanation: IPv6 eliminated broadcast entirely. NDP relies on multicast (notably the solicited-node multicast group), which is more targeted than ARP's flood.

lessons.title: The IPv4 Header, Field by Field
lessons.content:
IP is the **thin waist** of the Internet: everything rides over IP, and IP rides over everything. The IPv4 header is normally 20 bytes (no options) and you should be able to read it from a hex dump the way you once read a radar pulse's framing bits.

Walking the header top to bottom:

- **Version (4 bits)** = 4 for IPv4.
- **IHL (4 bits)** = header length in 32-bit words. Minimum value 5 (20 bytes); larger only when **options** are present.
- **DSCP/ECN (8 bits)** = the old Type of Service byte. The top 6 bits (DSCP) mark QoS class; the low 2 bits (ECN) let routers signal congestion *without dropping*.
- **Total Length (16 bits)** = entire datagram (header + payload) in bytes; caps a single datagram at 65,535.
- **Identification / Flags / Fragment Offset** = the fragmentation machinery (next lesson). Flags include **DF** (Don't Fragment) and **MF** (More Fragments).
- **TTL (8 bits)** = decremented at every hop; hits zero and the packet is discarded with an ICMP message. This is what stops L3 routing loops.
- **Protocol (8 bits)** = what's inside: 1=ICMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP.
- **Header Checksum (16 bits)** = covers the header *only*; because TTL changes each hop, every router recomputes it.
- **Source / Destination Address (32 bits each)**.
- **Options + padding** = rare, security/diagnostics; their presence is why IHL exists.

Note what is **not** here: no sequence numbers, no acknowledgements, no ports. IP is connectionless and best-effort. Reliability and process addressing are the transport layer's job.

items.front: How long is an IPv4 header with no options, and what field tells you its length?
items.back: 20 bytes with no options. The IHL (Internet Header Length) field gives the length in 32-bit words: minimum 5 (5 x 4 = 20 bytes). IHL > 5 means options are present (up to 60 bytes total).
items.hint: IHL counts 32-bit words.

items.front: What does the Protocol field identify, and give three common values.
items.back: It identifies the encapsulated upper-layer payload so the receiver knows how to demultiplex it. Common values: 1 = ICMP, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP.
items.hint: It points at the next header up the stack.

items.front: The IPv4 header checksum covers what, and why must every router recompute it?
items.back: It covers the header only, not the payload. Because TTL is decremented at every hop, the header changes, so each router must recompute the checksum. (TCP/UDP carry their own checksums covering the data.)
items.hint: TTL changes each hop.

items.front: What is the DSCP/ECN byte, and what was it formerly called?
items.back: Formerly the Type of Service (ToS) byte. The high 6 bits are DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) for QoS classification; the low 2 bits are ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification), letting routers signal congestion by marking packets instead of dropping them.
items.hint: 6 bits class + 2 bits congestion.

items.prompt: An IPv4 header's first byte is 0x45. What does this tell you?
choices: Version 4, IHL 5 (a 20-byte header with no options)
Version 5, with DSCP set to expedited forwarding
TTL of 69 hops remaining
Protocol 69, an experimental transport
items.explanation: The high nibble (4) is the Version and the low nibble (5) is the IHL. IHL=5 means 5 x 32-bit words = 20 bytes, i.e. no options. 0x45 is the canonical first byte of an ordinary IPv4 packet.

items.prompt: Which of these is NOT carried in the IPv4 header?
choices: Source port number
Time To Live
Protocol number
Destination address
items.explanation: Ports live in the transport header (TCP/UDP), not in IP. IP addresses hosts; ports address processes. This separation is exactly the connectionless, best-effort nature of IP: it has no notion of a connection or a process endpoint.

items.prompt: What is the maximum value of the Total Length field, and what does it bound?
choices: 65,535 bytes, the size of a single IP datagram (header + payload)
1,500 bytes, the Ethernet MTU
1,460 bytes, the TCP maximum segment size
4,294,967,295 bytes, since it is a 32-bit counter
items.explanation: Total Length is 16 bits, so the maximum datagram is 2^16 - 1 = 65,535 bytes including the header. The 1,500-byte limit is the link-layer MTU, a separate constraint that drives fragmentation.

items.prompt: Why does IP provide no acknowledgements, sequence numbers, or retransmission?
choices: IP is connectionless and best-effort by design; reliability is delegated to the transport layer (TCP) or the application
Those features were removed in IPv4 to save header space and never existed
Routers strip them to speed up forwarding
They are present but only used when the DF flag is set
items.explanation: IP deliberately offers only best-effort, connectionless delivery: it will try to deliver a datagram but makes no guarantees about ordering, duplication, or loss. The end-to-end principle pushes reliability to the edges (TCP), keeping the network core simple and fast.

lessons.title: Fragmentation, MTU & Path MTU Discovery
lessons.content:
Every link has a **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** — the largest payload its frames can carry. Classic Ethernet is 1500 bytes. When an IP datagram is larger than the next link's MTU, IPv4 can **fragment** it.

Fragmentation uses three header fields together:

- **Identification** — all fragments of one original datagram share this value, so the destination can group them.
- **Flags** — **MF** (More Fragments) is set on every fragment except the last; **DF** (Don't Fragment) forbids fragmentation entirely.
- **Fragment Offset** — where this fragment's data sits in the original, measured in 8-byte units.

Reassembly happens **only at the final destination**, never at intermediate routers, and if any fragment is lost the whole datagram is dropped. Fragmentation is therefore avoided today: it hurts performance and is a security headache.

The modern alternative is **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD)**: the sender sets **DF=1** and sends a large packet. If a router along the path has a smaller MTU, it drops the packet and returns an **ICMP "Fragmentation Needed"** message stating the link MTU. The sender shrinks its packets and retries.

PMTUD's notorious failure is the **PMTU black hole**: a misconfigured firewall silently blocks the ICMP Fragmentation-Needed messages. The sender never learns to shrink, keeps sending oversized DF packets that keep getting dropped, and the connection stalls. This is endemic in **VPNs and tunnels**, where encapsulation overhead shrinks the effective MTU. The classic symptom — small packets (ping, SSH login) work but large transfers (file copy, web pages) hang — is the giveaway that you are chasing a PMTU/fragmentation problem, not a routing or DNS problem.

items.front: What is an MTU, and what is the typical Ethernet value?
items.back: Maximum Transmission Unit: the largest payload a link's frame can carry. Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. A datagram larger than the next-hop MTU must be fragmented or rejected (if DF is set).
items.hint: Largest payload one frame can carry.

items.front: Which three IPv4 fields cooperate to perform fragmentation and reassembly?
items.back: Identification (shared by all fragments of one datagram), Flags (MF = More Fragments on all but the last; DF = Don't Fragment), and Fragment Offset (position in the original, in 8-byte units).
items.hint: ID groups them, offset orders them, MF marks the end.

items.front: Where are IP fragments reassembled, and what happens if one is lost?
items.back: Only at the final destination host — never at intermediate routers. If any single fragment is lost, the destination cannot reassemble and discards the entire datagram, forcing the upper layer to resend all of it.
items.hint: Endpoints only; all-or-nothing.
